قالت ارسل النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم غداه عاش عاشوراء الى قرى الانصار من اصبح مفطرا فليتم بقيه يومه ومن اصبح صائما فليصم قالت فكنا نصومه بعد ونصوم صبياننا ونجعل لهم اللعبه من العهن العهن يعني الصوف فاذا days up from following days he says but if the person who is ill and the person who is traveling if these if ‏the sick person the traveler do happ to fast it is acceptable because for them it is ومن كان مريضا او على سفر فعده من ايام اخر فان صام المريض والمسافر اجزاءهما لان اباحه الفطر لهما رخصه فان اخذ بالعزيمه فهو خير ان اخذ بالعزيمه يعني بالوجوب ان اخذ بالعزيمه يعني وصام حتى ولو كان معذورين فهو خير الله 184 الله سبحانه وتعالى ‏as those who are any is on trav inav on jy they are to make those days up from following days he says but if the person who is ill and the person who is traveling if these if ‏the sick person the traveler do happ to fast it is acceptable because for them it is allowance they are allowed to break their fast they don't have to and so if they choose to fast that is permissible so we will stop there and tomorrow we will prefering نسال الله سبحانه وتعالى ان يعلمنا ما ينفعنا وان ينفعنا بما علمنا وان يزيدنا علما والله اعلم وصلى الله وسلم على نبينا محمد وعلى اله واصحابه اجمعين.

summary: This lecture discusses Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) related to fasting (sawm) during Ramadan, specifically addressing who is obligated to fast and exemptions. The speaker covers rulings on accountability using the hadith of the Prophet Muhammad that lifts the pen of accountability from three types of people: the insane until they recover, the sleeping until they awaken, and children until they reach puberty. The lecture emphasizes that guardians should encourage children to practice fasting when able, and discusses exemptions for the sick and travelers, who can make up missed fasts on other days. The teaching references Quranic verses and authenticated hadith collections including Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
suggested_title: Fiqh of Fasting in Ramadan: Obligations, Exemptions, and Children's Practice
catchy_title: Who Must Fast? Islamic Rules for Fasting
